polymorphic-collections.label: Polymorphic Collections
polymorphic-collections.summary: Process a mixed list of objects through shared method names instead of type-specific branches.

sketch2.title: Polymorphic Collections: what the grader checks

try_polymorphic_collections_sketch0.title: Try it yourself
try_polymorphic_collections_sketch0.prompt: Complete the classes so `main.py` can call `.label()` on both `Book` and `Video` objects.

try_polymorphic_collections_sketch1.title: Try it yourself
try_polymorphic_collections_sketch1.prompt: Complete `total_fees()` so it calls `fee()` on every item without checking its class name.

ci-notification-polymorphism-practice.title: Send mixed notifications
ci-notification-polymorphism-practice.prompt: Build two notification classes with a shared `send()` method and use them polymorphically.
ci-notification-polymorphism-practice.hint: Read the imports first, then put each responsibility in the file named by that import.

help.concept: In a multi-file project, each file should own one clear responsibility and imports should connect those responsibilities.
help.hint_1: Run from the entry file and follow the first failing import or assertion.
help.hint_2: Keep the full project state working, not just the one line you changed.

ci-notification-polymorphism-practice.starterCode:
from models.email_notice import EmailNotice
from models.sms_notice import SmsNotice

notices = [EmailNotice("Ava", "Welcome"), SmsNotice("555-0101", "Ready")]
for notice in notices:
    print(notice.send())

sc-polymorphic-collections-design.title: Check the design choice
sc-polymorphic-collections-design.prompt: Which choice best matches the lesson goal for polymorphic collections?
sc-polymorphic-collections-design.hint: Choose the option that keeps object responsibilities clear.

help.concept: Process a mixed list of objects through shared method names instead of type-specific branches.
help.hint_1: Look for the answer that reduces duplication without hiding behavior.
help.hint_2: A good OOP answer usually keeps behavior near the object that owns the data.

options.a: Put every class in main.py so imports are never needed.
options.b: Give each class or service a focused responsibility and import it where needed.
options.c: Avoid methods so every object exposes all of its data directly.
options.d: Copy the same logic into each subclass to avoid shared code.

try-polymorphic-collections-sketch0.title: Loop over mixed media objects
try-polymorphic-collections-sketch0.prompt: Complete the classes so `main.py` can call `.label()` on both `Book` and `Video` objects.
try-polymorphic-collections-sketch0.hint: Read the imports first, then put each responsibility in the file named by that import.

help.concept: In a multi-file project, each file should own one clear responsibility and imports should connect those responsibilities.
help.hint_1: Run from the entry file and follow the first failing import or assertion.
help.hint_2: Keep the full project state working, not just the one line you changed.

try-polymorphic-collections-sketch0.starterCode:
from models.book import Book
from models.video import Video

items = [Book("Python", "Ana"), Video("OOP", 8)]
for item in items:
    print(item.label())

try-polymorphic-collections-sketch1.title: Write a service for a polymorphic list
try-polymorphic-collections-sketch1.prompt: Complete `total_fees()` so it calls `fee()` on every item without checking its class name.
try-polymorphic-collections-sketch1.hint: Read the imports first, then put each responsibility in the file named by that import.

help.concept: In a multi-file project, each file should own one clear responsibility and imports should connect those responsibilities.
help.hint_1: Run from the entry file and follow the first failing import or assertion.
help.hint_2: Keep the full project state working, not just the one line you changed.

try-polymorphic-collections-sketch1.starterCode:
from models.physical_book import PhysicalBook
from models.dvd import DVD
from services.catalog import total_fees

items = [PhysicalBook("SQL", 2), DVD("History", 3)]
print(total_fees(items))

concept.title: Polymorphic Collections: the idea
concept.bodyMarkdown: **Polymorphic Collections** is not just syntax. In this course it is practiced inside small projects where one file owns one clear part of the program. Process a mixed list of objects through shared method names instead of type-specific branches.

multifile-shape.title: Polymorphic Collections: the file shape
multifile-shape.bodyMarkdown: The file layout matters. Models hold object state and behavior, services coordinate groups of objects, tests verify behavior, and `main.py` stays thin enough to read.

graded-habit.title: Polymorphic Collections: what the grader checks
graded-habit.bodyMarkdown: The grader checks behavior from the running project. A strong answer keeps imports working, keeps all files present, and proves the object behavior through output or assertions.
